text: The surgeon performs a single port laparoscopic ileocolic resection, elevating the iliocolic pedicle and conducting a medial to lateral dissection to reach the root of the pedicle. A loop of ileum is visible, densely adherent, possibly forming a fistula to the right lower quadrant anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and prepares to take the ileocolic pedicle. The use of electrocautery is emphasized to control bleeding due to the unique angles and instrumentation of the single port laparoscopic approach.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection after dividing the pedicle, followed by a lateral dissection to mobilize the hepatic flexure. The ureter is checked and identified, visible moving in the surgical field. The goal is to exteriorize the specimen for hand-sewn extracorporeal anastomosis through the fan and steel incision.
The surgeon mobilizes the root of the terminal ileum (TI) towards the duodenum, working underneath the ileocolic pedicle to facilitate the small bowel's reach to the port.

The surgeon uses a single port access device with two instruments, Le Goucher and Parkes Grasper, to continue dissection. The camera view occasionally fogs due to smoke, which is cleared by pulling back into the port. The dissection progresses to join the previously medially dissected space.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the colon up and around the hepatic flexure to gain length for the ileocolic resection, ensuring the cecum can be exteriorized. The procedure is performed using a suprapubic single port approach, which is advantageous in patients with a favorable body habitus.
The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure, applying tension to the surrounding tissues to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure, allowing the bowel to be positioned for a hand-sewn anastomosis. The flexure is lowered, and the bowel is exteriorized for the procedure.
